title: PRINCIPLES AND GUIDELINES FOR THE EXCHANGE OF INFORMATION IN FOOD SAFETY EMERGENCY SITUATIONS

sections.section: PRINCIPLES AND GUIDELINES FOR THE EXCHANGE OF INFORMATION IN FOOD SAFETY EMERGENCY SITUATIONS
sections.summary: Heading/title for the Codex guidelines on principles and procedures for exchanging information in food safety emergency situations (no substantive text in this section).
Document identification noting CAC/GL 19-1995 with adoption in 1995 and revisions in 2004 and 2016 and an amendment in 2013.

Preamble stating that timely communication among competent authorities during food safety emergencies is essential to minimize public health impact and enable prompt action while avoiding unwarranted measures against unaffected batches, and that these Guidelines should be read with related Codex texts and FAO/WHO resources, noting INFOSAN as a mechanism for international information exchange.

Guidance for responding to food safety emergencies requiring competent authorities to communicate risks and coordinate timely action—applies when a hazard/product is identified or when scientific information links a food to serious health effects, includes feed when it may result in unsafe food, covers incidents involving imported/exported food (and feed for food-producing animals), and excludes routine import rejections for non‑compliance (covered by CAC/GL 25-1997).

Defines 'Food Safety Emergency' as a situation identified by a competent authority as a serious uncontrolled foodborne public‑health risk requiring urgent action and 'Food Safety Emergency Response' as the time‑constrained process of risk assessment, risk management and risk communication; notes 'feed' refers to feed and feed ingredients per CAC/RCP 54-2004 and cites related FAO/WHO guidance.

Sets principles for emergency response: many food safety emergencies may have international implications and be reportable to WHO/IHR/INFOSAN, information exchange should follow Codex risk analysis principles, and risk management measures may need to be taken on limited information due to time constraints.

Specifies key principles requiring each country to designate a primary official contact point; to provide clear, complete information (including risk assessments when available) via the most rapid effective channels in an agreed language; to promptly notify likely affected and exporting countries; to share all relevant information to support risk assessment/management/communication; to provide timely factual information to stakeholders; to maintain transparent continuous information flow; and to prohibit placing unsafe or unsuitable food into international trade for disposal.
Heading only: introduces the stakeholders section and has no substantive provisions itself.

Assigns competent authorities responsibility for managing and communicating food safety emergencies per paragraph 4.2, including prompt notification of official contact points (e.g. INFOSAN Emergency Contact Point) and other affected countries, verification and correction of scientific information to support risk processes, updating receiving countries on actions taken, and providing industry, consumers and stakeholders with timely, targeted information via a prepared communication plan (including guidance for sensitive groups).

Describes INFOSAN as the FAO/WHO network for global dissemination of urgent food safety information: the INFOSAN Emergency Contact Point reports events to the Secretariat, INFOSAN maintains national official contact points, liaises with authorities to collect/validate/share factual information during incidents, and is a key international resource (with some events potentially reportable under IHR 2005).

States that food business operators bear primary responsibility for ensuring food safety and must contribute to emergency management by maintaining effective traceability systems, providing timely information and records (preferably digitally searchable) to competent authorities and stakeholders, training staff and communicating internally; these obligations also apply to feed business operators when feed is implicated.
Advises consumers to protect their health by staying informed and following instructions from competent authorities during food safety emergencies.

sections.section: 6 PROCESSES FOR FOOD SAFETY EMERGENCY RESPONSE
sections.summary: Heading for processes for food safety emergency response that refers readers to relevant sections of the FAO/WHO guide on applying risk analysis principles and procedures during emergencies.

Requires the competent authority to develop a national food safety emergency plan that sets procedures (including communication) and responsibilities for coordination among parties, referencing the FAO/WHO framework for developing such plans.

sections.section: 6.2 Designated official contact points for information exchange
sections.summary: Requires each country to designate a primary official contact point as the national focal point for food safety emergency information exchange, allows designation of specific contact points for particular emergencies, and requires updated primary contact information be provided to INFOSAN.

Directs competent authorities to consider the level of distribution (wholesale, retail, consumer), quantity, and transit status of implicated food/feed when implementing risk management and communication measures (including recalls at one or more distribution levels) and references FAO/WHO guidance on recall systems; if product has not entered an importing country, measures focus on importers/border control, whereas distributed consignments may require coordinated measures by exporting and importing authorities.

Obliges the identifying competent authority, working with others, to determine all potential recipient and source countries for implicated food/feed and to provide them with all relevant information rapidly using expedient channels with receipt verification; to identify affected foods/feed in as much detail as available; to supplement initial incomplete notifications as more data emerge; and to issue clear, timely status updates to stakeholders.

Recommends using a standard format for information exchange between importing and exporting countries while allowing competent authorities to limit exchanged information according to national privacy laws, and notes a model format is provided in the Annex.

States that initial communications may include presumptions and precautionary measures and should be refined as more detail becomes available, and that transparent communications between designated official contact points must continue through all phases (including risk assessments) until resolution to allow ongoing review of risk strategies.
Encourages consideration of setting up early warning systems for food safety and notes the FAO EMPRES Food Safety programme may assist in establishing such systems.

sections.section: STANDARD FORMAT FOR INFORMATION EXCHANGE IN FOOD SAFETY EMERGENCIES
sections.summary: Heading for the standard format for information exchange that states initial information should be exchanged as quickly as possible even if incomplete, with further details to follow as they become available.

sections.section: 1. Nature of the food safety emergency
sections.summary: Specifies that notifications should describe the nature of the hazard (biological, chemical, physical, radionuclide, undeclared allergen, processing faults, or unknown agent), identify the specific agent and its level or prevalence, report sampling and analytical methods and assumptions, and describe associated adverse health effects and epidemiological data (e.g. incubation period, severity).

sections.section: 2. Identification of foods or, as appropriate, feeds concerned
sections.summary: Requires detailed identification of affected foods/feeds, including product description and quantity (brand, label name, grade, preservation, shelf life), package type/size, lot identification (codes, production/processing dates, premises), other marks (bar/UPC), producer/manufacturer/packer/exporter/importer details, pictorial image, export certificate references, and the countries to which the product has been exported when known.

sections.section: 3. Affected or potentially affected population group(s)
sections.summary: In food safety emergencies, identify and communicate any affected or potentially affected vulnerable population groups (e.g., children, pregnant women, immunocompromised persons, elderly).

Provide, where available, exporter and importer names and contact details, container and shipping information including ports of origin/destination, applicable HS/tariff codes, and consignee/shipper identities and contacts.

sections.section: 5. Action taken by exporting or importing country
sections.summary: Describe actions taken by the exporting or importing country, including measures to identify/prevent sale and export, voluntary or mandatory recalls, prevention measures, physical treatments to reduce risk, diagnosis/treatment of affected persons, final disposition (e.g., destruction), laboratory analyses, and any other relevant information.

sections.section: 6. Details of the designated primary official contact point and of the relevant competent authority
sections.summary: Provide full contact details for the designated primary official and competent authority (name, address, telephone, email, fax and responsible office/person) and a website where available for up‑to‑date information.
